description:
My favorite Sore-mouth Bush (Palicourea tomentosa) observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?verifiable=any&taxon_id=1145089&preferred_place_id=917&user_id=aparrot1

Blue Fruits: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/237586271

Sore-mouth Bush (Palicourea tomentosa), a.k.a. Hot Lips, is tropical plant in the Madder (Rubiaceae) family. Bracts are bright red, resembling painted lips, and tomentose (fuzzy). Fruits are dull blue.

Sore-mouth Bush (Palicourea tomentosa) A.k.a. Hot Lips (Psychotria poeppigiana). The bright red bracts are tomentose. A.K.A. Palicourea tomentosa. It is one of the characteristic shrubs of the Neoptropics and ranges widely through Tropical America. It is a member of the family Rubiaceae (coffee family) and belongs to a genus containing nearly two thousand species worldwide. The opposing bright red bracts of the flowering stalk look like giant ruby-red lips, hence its racy local name “labios ardientes.” The actual flowers are small, yellow and protrude between the “lips.”
The plants are visited by a number of butterflies and hummingbirds. The pea-sized fruit are bright blue. The opposite leaves, the stems and flower bracts are all covered in a soft, hairy fuzz. Mature, robust specimens may reach small tree size, but most encountered are small shrubs under six feet tall." https://canopytower.com/hot-lips/

"Palicourea tomentosa is a large shrub. The inflorescences are carried upright or semi-erect and are surrounded by large bracts, colored a conspicuous red, that attract pollinators. The flowers themselves are inconspicuous, with the small yellow petals and sepals forming a narrow corollar tube. Pollinators are mainly hummingbirds, namely small hermit (Phaethornithinae) species like the black-throated hermit (Phaethornis atrimentalis), straight-billed hermit (P. bourcieri) and reddish hermit (P. ruber). They do not insert their bills deeply into the small flowers, and thus the pollinators of the sore-mouth bush include curved- and straight-billed species."
https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1145089-Palicourea-tomentosa

"There are two species of kissing lips, the glabrous is Palicourea elata which is found from low elevations up to montane cloud forests, and the hairy is Palicourea tomentosa restricted to the lower areas. Because of the hairs they are sometimes called male and female" INat andreas_berger https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/153353586

Sore-mouth Bush (Palicourea tomentosa) is ia member of Rubioideae. Rubioideae is a subfamily of flowering plants in the Madder (Rubiaceae) family. https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/788064-Rubioideae

"Genera In Family: +- 500 genera, 6000 species worldwide, especially in the tropics. Many are cultivated, including Coffee, Cinchona, Quinine, and many ornamentals.Habit: Annual to tree, or vine. Leaf: generally opposite (whorled), entire; stipules generally fused to stem, adjacent pairs occasionally fused, or occasionally leaf-like and appearing like whorled leaves. Inflorescence: cyme, panicle, spike, cluster, or flower 1, generally terminal and +- axillary. Flower: generally bisexual; calyx +- 4(5)-lobed, occasionally 0 (Galium, Crucianella) or 6 (Sherardia); corolla generally radial, 4(5)-lobed; stamens epipetalous*, alternate corolla lobes, generally included; ovary generally inferior, chambers generally 2 or 4, style 1(2). Fruit: drupe, berry, or 2 or 4 nutlets [capsule]. " https://ucjeps.berkeley.edu/eflora/eflora_display.php?tid=242

Note: "Epipetalous" means stamens that are attached to the petals of a flower. For example, the flowers of the Solanaceae family (Nightshades) have epipetalous stamens.

Madder (Rubiaceae) family:
"Rubiaceae (/ruːbiˈeɪsiːˌiː, -siˌaɪ/) is a family of flowering plants, commonly known as the coffee, madder, or bedstraw family. It consists of terrestrial trees, shrubs, lianas, or herbs that are recognizable by simple, opposite leaves with interpetiolar stipules and sympetalous actinomorphic flowers. The family contains about 14,100 species in about 580 genera, which makes it the fourth-largest angiosperm family. Rubiaceae has a cosmopolitan distribution; however, the largest species diversity is concentrated in the tropics and subtropics." https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/47693-Rubiaceae

Sore-mouth Bush (Palicourea tomentosa), a.k.a. Hot Lips, is tropical plant in the Madder (Rubiaceae) family. Bracts are bright red, resembling painted lips, and tomentose (fuzzy). Fruits are dull blue.

Sore-mouth Bush (Palicourea tomentosa) A.k.a. Hot Lips (Psychotria poeppigiana). The bright red bracts are tomentose. A.K.A. Palicourea tomentosa. It is one of the characteristic shrubs of the Neoptropics and ranges widely through Tropical America. It is a member of the family Rubiaceae (coffee family) and belongs to a genus containing nearly two thousand species worldwide. The opposing bright red bracts of the flowering stalk look like giant ruby-red lips, hence its racy local name “labios ardientes.” The actual flowers are small, yellow and protrude between the “lips.”
The plants are visited by a number of butterflies and hummingbirds. The pea-sized fruit are bright blue. The opposite leaves, the stems and flower bracts are all covered in a soft, hairy fuzz. Mature, robust specimens may reach small tree size, but most encountered are small shrubs under six feet tall." https://canopytower.com/hot-lips/

"Palicourea tomentosa is a large shrub. The inflorescences are carried upright or semi-erect and are surrounded by large bracts, colored a conspicuous red, that attract pollinators. The flowers themselves are inconspicuous, with the small yellow petals and sepals forming a narrow corollar tube. Pollinators are mainly hummingbirds, namely small hermit (Phaethornithinae) species like the black-throated hermit (Phaethornis atrimentalis), straight-billed hermit (P. bourcieri) and reddish hermit (P. ruber). They do not insert their bills deeply into the small flowers, and thus the pollinators of the sore-mouth bush include curved- and straight-billed species."
https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1145089-Palicourea-tomentosa

"There are two species of kissing lips, the glabrous is Palicourea elata which is found from low elevations up to montane cloud forests, and the hairy is Palicourea tomentosa restricted to the lower areas. Because of the hairs they are sometimes called male and female" INat andreas_berger https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/153353586

Sore-mouth Bush (Palicourea tomentosa) is ia member of Rubioideae. Rubioideae is a subfamily of flowering plants in the Madder (Rubiaceae) family. https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/788064-Rubioideae

description:
Este individuo lleva desde inicios de julio de 2025 hasta estos momentos (enero de 2026) donde llega a un arbusto espinoso en la ventana de mi cuarto para dormir. Llega a las 6:40 PM y se retira a las 6:40 AM (aproximadamente) no tiene un horario exacto entre las 6:43 am para levantarse o 6:50 pm para acostarse dónde antes de acostarse suele mostrar un comportamiento un poco alterado donde vuela y se posa repetidamente en la rama del arbusto y moviendo mucho la cola cuando esta posado hasta que se queda en estado de topor.

This individual has been returning to the same thorny bush outside my bedroom window to sleep since early July 2025 (continuing through January 2026). Its routine is very consistent, arriving around 6:40 PM and departing around 6:40 AM, with slight variations between 6:50 PM for settling in and 6:43 AM for waking up. Before roosting, it displays a somewhat restless behavior, repeatedly flying and perching on the branches while constantly flicking its tail. It continues this until it finally enters a state of torpor for the night.

The opposing bright red bracts of the flowering stalk look like giant ruby-red lips, hence its racy local name “labios ardientes.” The actual flowers are small, yellow and protrude between the “lips.”
The plants are visited by a number of butterflies and hummingbirds. The pea-sized fruit are bright blue. The opposite leaves, the stems and flower bracts are all covered in a soft, hairy fuzz. Mature, robust specimens may reach small tree size, but most encountered are small shrubs under six feet tall." https://canopytower.com/hot-lips/

"Palicourea tomentosa is a large shrub. The inflorescences are carried upright or semi-erect and are surrounded by large bracts, colored a conspicuous red, that attract pollinators. The flowers themselves are inconspicuous, with the small yellow petals and sepals forming a narrow corollar tube. Pollinators are mainly hummingbirds, namely small hermit (Phaethornithinae) species like the black-throated hermit (Phaethornis atrimentalis), straight-billed hermit (P. bourcieri) and reddish hermit (P. ruber). They do not insert their bills deeply into the small flowers, and thus the pollinators of the sore-mouth bush include curved- and straight-billed species."
https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1145089-Palicourea-tomentosa

"There are two species of kissing lips, the glabrous is Palicourea elata which is found from low elevations up to montane cloud forests, and the hairy is Palicourea tomentosa restricted to the lower areas. My favorite Green-breasted Mango (Anthracothorax prevostii) observations: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?verifiable=any&taxon_id=5798&preferred_place_id=917&user_id=aparrot1
Closeup in Costa Rica: https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/273509459


Green-breasted Mango (Anthracothorax prevostii) is a large, stocky hummingbird with a thick, arched, black bill in the Mango Hummingbirds (Anthracothorax) genus. The male has a stunning purple tail which it flares in flight. It is found from eastern Mexico south through most of Central America, in Colombia and Venezuela, and as a vagrant in the United States. There are several subspecies.
Anthracothorax genus mangos are large hummingbirds found mainly at the middle and upper levels of forest edge, second growth, and woodland. All have a slightly decurved bill and magenta-purple tail. Green-breasted mango is the most common and widespread of this genus.

Birds of Central America, A. Vallely and D. Dyer, Princeton University Press, 2018, pp. 94-95

Birds of Costa Rica, Dale Dyer and Steve N. G. Howell, Princeton Field Guides, 2023, pp. 216-217

Ebird with species description, range map and sound recordings: https://ebird.org/species/gnbman
Green-breasted Mango (Anthracothorax prevostii) "avors forest edge and open areas with scattered tall trees. Often perches and feeds high in canopy, but also feeds low on occasions and will visit feeders. Male (some females similar) solidly dark, deep green overall with black throat, purplish tail. Female and immature have broad dark stripe down center of throat and breast, white tail corners. Immature also has rusty mottling along sides of throat and breast."

"Hummingbirds showcase a dizzying array of plumages, almost all of which rely on odd tail plumes or the angle-sensitive iridescence of their feathers for their effect. The extreme maneuverability of these most acrobatic of birds seems to engender a lack of concern about predators, making them easy to approach near sources of food. Burning energy so fast during the day that most species cannot make it through the night on a stomach full of nectar, most go into torpor every night, even in tropical environments, to reduce energy loss.
Hummingbirds occur in a wide variety of habitats across the New World, from arid deserts, to dense tropical rainforests, to high Andean scrub near glaciers and snowfields.
Hummingbirds feed on nectar and small insects and other invertebrates. Although nectar is most important for all species, hummingbirds are remarkably effective insectivores, capturing small flying prey with their bills."

Hummingbirds and Flowers: an INaturalist Project: https://www.inaturalist.org/projects/hummingbirds-and-flowers

Hummingbird Feeders: In addition to planting native plants for nectar, you can supplement hummingbird diet with a hummingbird feeder. 4:1 (or even 3:1) is a good ratio of water to sugar. Change the solution at least weekly before it gets cloudy or moldy, and NEVER add red dye!

Details

Levey, D. R., and G. C. Daily. 2025. “ Male-Like Plumage in an Urban Nesting Veraguan Mango: Evidence of a Female-Limited Polymorphism?.” Ecology and Evolution 15, no. 9: e72071. https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.72071.

description:
Nota de campo

Registro de una hembra de Picaflor Frente Azul observado en la reserva natural Ecoportal de Piedra, dentro de la ecorregión de las Yungas, Jujuy, Argentina.

El avistaje ocurrió durante una caminata por un sendero en selva de montaña (nuboselva), en un ambiente húmedo con presencia de niebla y vegetación densa característica del sotobosque de las Yungas. El individuo se posó brevemente en una pequeña rama antes de volver a desplazarse entre la vegetación.

La especie habita selvas montanas andinas y se alimenta principalmente de néctar y pequeños insectos, cumpliendo un rol importante como polinizador dentro de este ecosistema.

Fecha y hora del registro: 5 de septiembre de 2023 – 08:56 hs.

Datos de la toma:
Velocidad de obturación 1/750 s · Apertura f/5.6 · ISO 2500. 📷

The range of this beautiful bird is restricted to the highlands of eastern Panama and extreme northwestern Colombia. We connected with it in Cerro Azul - cool highlands above Panama City - more specifically at the feeders run by Karl Kaufman and Rosabel Miro (current president of the Panama Audubon Society).
Appears to be an imm. male.  Forked mostly rufousy tail can be seen in the additional  photographs

description: May 18th/first sighting of this species, at the same area as in July/August 2023 when the same nasturtium (harlingii) sprawled across the sunnier areas.

El pechiche (Vitex gigantea) es un árbol nativo de la región costera de Ecuador, conocido por sus hojas compuestas y flores pequeñas de color blanco o lila. Puede alcanzar alturas de hasta 20 metros y produce frutos drupáceos oscuros y redondeados que sirven de alimento para diversas especies de fauna, incluyendo aves y mamíferos. Este árbol juega un papel crucial en la preservación del colibrí Estrellita Esmeraldeña (Chaetocercus berlepschi), una especie en peligro de extinción que depende de los recursos proporcionados por el pechiche para su supervivencia.

En términos de mitigación del cambio climático, el pechiche captura dióxido de carbono a través de la fotosíntesis, almacenándolo en su biomasa. Además, su presencia en áreas costeras ayuda a proteger contra la erosión del suelo y mantener la estabilidad de los ecosistemas locales. Al proporcionar hábitats y recursos alimenticios para diversas especies, incluido el colibrí Estrellita Esmeraldeña, el pechiche contribuye a la biodiversidad y la resiliencia de los ecosistemas frente a los impactos del cambio climático.

From July 15, the first day to observe this species at the 'Fairy Garden' of nasturtiums, until mid August,  the woodstars there on every visit.. morning, noon, afternoon.  As long as the rains continued, so did the growth of the nasturtiums.  Five species of hummingbirds could be observed there - and usually  at least four or five woodstars.      Since these sightings are so rare for this time of the year, I ask the ornithologists:  Should I upload observations from every day I visited that site, or will the eBird data suffice for recording their respite at la Tomatera?

Adding this observation that shows two woodstars' behavior.. these medium-sized trees border the large area carpeted with the nasturtiums, and oftentimes the Amazilias also interact with the woodstars that hold that specific tree as their base..  Several more similar trees beside that one give them a view on each side of two different areas of the nasturtiums.

Six full weeks of seeing this species 'paired' with the nasturtiums;  for almost two weeks we have had no rainfall, so the vines have desiccated in most of the area.  The original 'site' is about half green and half desiccated, yet there were no Woodstars until after 6 pm - and just this one.     Also near was one Amazilia and a few bumblebees; the woodstar's buzzzzzz was slightly stronger than the bees'.     Other flowering plants are beginning to bloom (white plumbago, the pink endemic escoba hibiscus, etc), but so far only the Amazilias are scattered in the area that is returning to the normal - without the garnish of the pink nasturtiums.

A bit too far for good photos, this bird was in an area where Amazilia Hummingbird visits the Tropaeolum harlingii which is sprawling across the lower areas and also climbing to the tops of the smaller trees.

Three different people have looked at the photos and all said it is the Esmeraldas Woodstar.  Two said young male;  I look forward to any feedback.

The rains have prevented a return, but as soon as possible I will return to the location and sit/watch/observe and hope to see it again.

Visto en el colegio Emil Friedman.

description:
· Dominio: Eukaryota
· Reino: Plantae
· División: Magnoliophyta
· Clase: Magnoliopsida (Dicotiledóneas)
· Orden: Rosales
· Familia: Rosaceae (¡La misma familia de las rosas, manzanas y duraznos!)
· Género: Polylepis (Género endémico de los Andes)
· Especie: Polylepis incana

Polylepis significa "muchas escamas", en referencia a la corteza. Incana significa "grisáceo" o "canoso", describiendo el color de sus hojas por el envés.

Origen y Distribución Nativa

· Distribución Nativa y Endemismo: El género Polylepis es endémico de los Andes tropicales. Polylepis incana en particular es nativa de la región central y norte de los Andes.

· En Ecuador: Es nativa y una de las especies de Polylepis más comunes en los Andes ecuatorianos. Se distribuye en los páramos y bosques altoandinos, típicamente entre los 3,200 y 4,500 metros sobre el nivel del mar. Forma bosques puros (llamados "queñuales" o "bosques de Polylepis") que son ecosistemas únicos.

Morfología y Datos Curiosos 

Es conocido comúnmente como "Árbol de Papel", "Quiñua", "Quinual" o "Yagual".

· Porte: Árbol pequeño a mediano, de 3 a 10 metros de altura, con una copa irregular y retorcida, adaptada a los vientos fuertes y fríos.

· Corteza: Es exfoliante, formando múltiples capas delgadas como papel de color marrón rojizo que se desprenden en láminas. Esta corteza es un excelente aislante térmico que protege al árbol de las heladas extremas. Es una de las adaptaciones más notables al frío en el reino vegetal.

· Hojas: Son compuestas, pequeñas y coriáceas. El envés es densamente cubierto de pelos blancos o grisáceos (incana) que ayudan a reducir la pérdida de agua y a reflejar la radiación ultravioleta intensa de la alta montaña.

· Curiosidad Ecológica Extrema: Los bosques de Polylepis son considerados los bosques que crecen a mayor altitud del mundo. Son islas de biodiversidad y humedad en el páramo, actuando como "esponjas" que capturan el agua de la neblina y la liberan lentamente, alimentando las lagunas y quebradas.

Datos Culturales y Ecológicos en Ecuador

1. Guardián del Agua: Su rol ecológico más importante es la captación y regulación hídrica. Los bosques de Polylepis son cruciales para la provisión de agua para las ciudades andinas, la agricultura y los ecosistemas del páramo. Sin ellos, el páramo se secaría más rápido.

2. Refugio de Biodiversidad Única: Estos bosques albergan especies endémicas y amenazadas que no viven en ningún otro lugar, como el Colibrí Estrella de Garganta Azul (Oreotrochilus cyanolaemus) y la Coneja de Páramo (Sylvilagus andinus). Son hotspots de avifauna.

3. Madera y Leña Tradicional: Su madera es muy densa y excelente como leña y carbón (arde por mucho tiempo y da buen calor). Históricamente, su corteza se usó como aislante en construcciones y su madera para herramientas.

4. Amenaza Crítica y Esfuerzos de Conservación: Los bosques de Polylepis están severamente fragmentados y reducidos debido a siglos de tala para leña, expansión agrícola y quema del páramo. Es una especie amenazada. Su conservación y restauración son una prioridad nacional.

5. Símbolo de Resiliencia Andina: Por su capacidad de sobrevivir en condiciones extremas, el árbol de Polylepis es un símbolo de fortaleza, adaptación y persistencia. Representa la lucha por la conservación del frágil ecosistema del páramo.

Conclusión

Polylepis incana (Quiñua/Árbol de Papel) es mucho más que un árbol nativo: es una especie ingeniera de ecosistemas, clave para la vida en los Andes y en grave peligro. 

Es el arquitecto de los bosques altoandinos, un garante del agua para millones de personas y un santuario de biodiversidad única. Su conservación es sinónimo de seguridad hídrica y salud del páramo ecuatoriano. Es, sin duda, uno de los patrimonios naturales más valiosos y vulnerables del país.
